rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RAYMOND L. MAR REVOCABLE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Inspectin Demand, Set One; And For A Monetary Sanction
rulings.Rulings: Tentative Ruling by Judge Ross C. Moody (Part 1 of 3) In the RAYMOND L. MAR REVOCABLE TRUST dated June 5, 1990, and Restated on September 25, 2012, there are two petitions pending: Case No. PTR-19-302661, and Related Case No. PTR-19-302674. Gregory Mar is petitioner in PTR-19-302661, and his sisters Antoinette Mallette and Veronica Ligne Mar are respondents. In contrast, in PTR-19-302674, Antoinette Mallette and Veronica Ligne Mar are petitioners and Gregory Mar is respondent. Before the court are three motions to compel discovery: Motion to Compel Further Responses to Special Interrogatories, Set One. Motion to Compel Further Responses to Inspection Demand, Set One. Motion to Compel Further Responses to Form Interrogatories, Set Two. Each of the motions to compel also seeks monetary sanctions. For the reasons set forth below, the motions to compel further responses to special interrogatories and to the inspection demand are GRANTED (subject to an appropriate protective order, as described below) and the motion to compel further responses to form interrogatories is DENIED as moot. The requests for monetary sanctions are DENIED. In July of 2022, the consolidated petitions were sent to Judge Mary E. Wiss for trial. During pretrial proceedings, Judge Wiss discovered that no responsive pleading to the First Amended Petition for Determination of Rights in Real Property, etc. in PTR-19-302674 had been filed by Gregory Mar. Accordingly, by order dated January 17, 2023, she declared PTR-19-302674 "not at issue," vacated the trial date, returned the consolidated cases to Department 204 for further proceedings. When the consolidated petitions were heard in Department 204 on March 27, 2023, Gregory Mar was ordered to file a responsive pleading in PTR-19-302674 on or before April 10, 2023. Thereafter, Gregory Mar submitted a responsive pleading on or about April 11, 2023. (The document was apparently prepared for filing and transmitted to counsel on April 11, and later was processed by the clerk with a filing date of April 17, 2023.) One feature of the responsive pleading was that the verification page revealed that Gregory Mar recently suffered a stroke, and explained that certain aspects of the pleading could not be fully vetted by him: "I am the Respondent in the instant matter, Case no. PTR-19-302674. I recently had a stroke and I am unable to read the foregoing GREOGRY MAR'S RESPONSES AND OBJECTIONS TO FIRST AMENDED PETITION FOR A DETERMINATION OF RIGHTS IN REAL PROPERTY AND REMOVAL OF CO-TRUSTEE. I have been informed of its contents and certify that the same is true and correct of my own personal knowledge, expect for matters stated therein on information and belief, and with respect to those matters I believe them to be true." (Verification, p. 14.) After receiving the responses and objections with the carve-out for health concerns related to the stroke, Counsel for Antoinette Mallette and Veronica Ligne Mar issued discovery focused on the stroke, Gregory Mar's health status, and the general veracity of the stroke claim. Three discovery devices were employed, and all three are the subject of this tentative ruling. (end of part 1)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RAYMOND L. MAR REVOCABLE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Further Responses To Inspection Demand, Set One; And For A Monetary Sanction (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (part 3 of 3) The court does not agree that information about Mr. Mar's recent stroke is irrelevant, nor does it believe that the information sought about it is moot. As pointed out above, Mr. Mar's recent pleadings disclose that he had a stroke sufficiently severe to render him unable to read in April. Moreover, his pleadings disclose that the stroke was sufficiently serious as to require hospitalization. In his amended response to the petition, Mr. Mar refers to his "current inability to provided responses" as a reason that "many of the responses" in the amended response are "limited." (Amended Response, p. 2.) The court agrees with movant that Mr. Mar's present health status has been injected in the case by his counsel as an explanation for defects in the written responses to the petition, and, on a going-forward basis, is identified as a reason why counsel may change the "limited" nature of the response to the allegations. Accordingly, the court will grant the motions to compel with respect to the discovery related to Mr. Mar's present health status, including details about his stroke, the treatment for it, and his ability to act as a trustee and occupant of the trust property and as a Respondent at any trial in the case. Due to the sensitive nature of the information about Mr. Mar's health, the court directs the parties to meet and confer about an appropriate protective order limiting the use of this discovery to this litigation, and to come together on a stipulated protective order within 7 days of the entry of this order. (See Movant's Reply Brief in Support of Motion to Compel Special Interrogatories, p. 6, l. 15-16; see also Manela v. Superior Court (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1139,1151 ["The trial court should further consider issuing a protective order limiting the dissemination of [] private medical records."]) Counsel are ordered to act in good faith with respect to this portion of the order, and may be subject to sanctions if they fail to do so. Based on the reasoning expressed above, the court GRANTS the Motion to Compel Further Responses to Special Interrogatories, Set One, and the Motion to Compel Further Responses to Inspection Demand. Verified, substantive and code-compliant responses to this discovery must be provided within 14 days after the stipulated protective order is in place. The court DENIES as moot the Motion to Compel Further Responses to Form Interrogatories, which was de-limited to a response to FI 1.1 in the reply brief. The Court denies all three requests for monetary sanctions. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. (end of tentative ruling)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE RAYMOND L. MAR REVOCABLE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Further Responses To Inspection Demand, Set One; And For A Monetary Sanction (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (part 2 of 3) First, an inspection demand was served on April 13, 2023. On May 18, 2023, unverified objection-only responses were received from Mr. Mar's attorney. Second, Form Interrogatories were served on April 13, 2023. On May 18, 2023, unverified objection-only responses were received from Mr. Mar's attorney. Finally, Special Interrogatories were served on April 13, 2023. On May 18, 2023, unverified objection-only responses were received from Mr. Mar's attorney. On June 27, 2023, after the instant discovery was served, and after the instant motions were filed, Mr. Mar filed an amended responsive pleading entitled GREGORY MAR'S AMENDED RESPONSES AND OBJECTIONS TO FIRST AMENDED PETITION FOR A DETERMINATION OF RIGHTS IN REAL PROPERTY AND REMOVAL OF CO-TRUSTEE. This document had a conventional verification under penalty of perjury with no reservation based on stroke. However, the document also contained the following paragraph: "On March 27, 2023, Respondent herein was ordered to file a response to this action on or before April 10, 2023. Respondent's attorney was on previously scheduled vacations from March 27 to April 2, and April 6 to April 10, and was unable to reach Mr. Mar on April 3, 4, or 5. It was later discovered that Mr. Mar was hospitalized due to a stroke. Because of the limited amount of time to respond, the unavailability of counsel, the unavailability of Respondent, and Respondent's current inability to provide responses, many of the responses herein are limited." (Amended Response, p. 2, emphasis added.) The instant discovery motions have some overlapping arguments. In response to all three motions, counsel for Mr. Mar asserts that discovery has closed. The argument is, in essence, that a trial date of November 28, 2022 was set for the consolidated cases, and that "[d]iscovery was therefore closed by law on October 27, 2022" citing Code of Civil Procedure section 2024.020(a). (Gregory Mar's MPA in Opposition to Motion to Compel Further Responses to Inspection Demand at pp. 2-3.) The court disagrees. As mentioned above, Judge Wiss found that PTR-19-302674 "not at issue" because no responsive pleading had been filed in response to the amended petition. It would make no sense for discovery to be closed in a matter before the answering pleading was filed since discovery regarding denials, admissions and affirmative defenses could not be undertaken prior to the filing of a response. The court rejects the contention that discovery is closed with respect to all three of the instant motions. The second overlapping argument presented in opposition to the instant motions is that the discovery regarding Mr. Mar's stroke violates his right to privacy. For example, in the MPA in Opposition to Motion to Compel Further Responses to Special Interrogatories, Set One, Mr. Mar points out that even if relevant, information about his health can only be ordered produced after a careful balancing of the need for discovery versus his "fundamental right of privacy" in information about his health. (Gregory Mar's MPA in Opposition to Motion to Compel Further Responses to Special Interrogatories, Set One at p. 4.) The Opposition then asserts that movant has only "proffered conjectures" about the relevance of the information sought. (Id., at p. 5.) Finally, the Opposition suggests that the discovery is moot "based on the amended response filed." (Id., at p. 6.) With respect to mootness, the argument advanced is that because the amendment either "admit[ed] each of the allegations or provid[ed] an explanation of why Mr. Mar lacked the necessary information to admit or deny the allegations," the "question raised in the special interrogatory is moot." (Id., at p. 7.) (end of part 2)
